steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has an opening em-dash before "the point in the sky from which..." That em-dash opens an appositive phrase defining "radiant." Em-dashes used to set off an interrupting phrase must come in matched pairs: the phrase must close with a second em-dash. The blank must supply the correct punctuation to close the appositive and let the main clause continue.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'emanate, is 39 degrees—': Places the closing em-dash after the predicate "is 39 degrees," which cuts off the main clause and leaves the sentence without a proper ending. The appositive would swallow the predicate. ✗
  • Choice B — 'emanate—is 39 degrees,': Places the closing em-dash immediately after "emanate," correctly ending the appositive. The main clause then reads: "The declination...is 39 degrees, meaning that..." — grammatically complete. The comma after "39 degrees" correctly introduces the elaborating phrase. ✓
  • Choice C — 'emanate is 39 degrees;': Uses no closing em-dash at all, leaving the opening em-dash unmatched and the sentence broken. A semicolon cannot close a dashed appositive. ✗
  • Choice D — 'emanate, is 39 degrees,': Uses commas instead of an em-dash to close the appositive, which violates the matching-em-dash rule. A comma cannot close a phrase opened by an em-dash. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
